assessment.summary: A geologically well-documented, quantified hazard for coastal subduction zones — USGS puts the chance of a full-margin ~M9 Cascadia earthquake at 10-15% in the next 50 years — capable of regional-scale destruction and a major tsunami with comparatively little warning time.
assessment.timeframe: USGS puts the chance of a full-margin ~M9 Cascadia Subduction Zone earthquake at 10-15% in the next 50 years, with much higher odds (85%) for a smaller but still damaging M6.5+ deep earthquake in the Puget Sound region over the same window.

claims.text: There is a 10-15-percent chance of an approximately magnitude 9 earthquake on the Cascadia Subduction Zone in the next 50 years.

There is an 85-percent chance of a magnitude 6.5 or greater deep earthquake in the Puget Sound region, and a 17-percent chance of a magnitude 6.5 or greater crustal fault earthquake in the Puget Sound region, in the next 50 years.

The Pacific Northwest earthquake hazard model identifies three types of earthquakes in the region: deep (intraslab) earthquakes, subduction zone (megathrust) earthquakes, and shallow crustal earthquakes.

description: A great (magnitude 8+) earthquake on a subduction-zone megathrust fault, where one tectonic plate slides beneath another, capable of rupturing hundreds of kilometers of coastline in a single event and generating a major ocean-crossing tsunami. Subduction zones such as Cascadia (US Pacific Northwest), the Nankai Trough (Japan), and Sumatra have produced the largest and most destructive earthquakes and tsunamis in recorded history.
name: Megathrust Earthquake and Tsunami
